file_verdicts.summary: Official binary, proper checksum, no malicious code

Cloning https://aur.archlinux.org/feather-wallet-bin.git to /tmp/aur-sleuth/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-cgdbxbad...

Analyzing feather-wallet-bin AUR package (working in /tmp/aur-sleuth/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-cgdbxbad) with qwen/qwen3-235b-a22b-2507 from https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines a standard binary package build process. The `source` array fetches a ZIP archive from the project's official GitHub releases page using a predictable URL based on `pkgver`. The hash for the archive is explicitly defined via `sha256sums` and does not use 'SKIP', ensuring the integrity of the downloaded file. The file is not extracted during the source retrieval phase due to `noextract`, which is safe and expected.

The PKGBUILD contains no top-level command substitutions, no `pkgver()` function (so default static `pkgver` is used), and no code executed outside of the `package()` function, which will not run due to the `--noprepare` and `--nobuild` flags. Therefore, sourcing the PKGBUILD during `makepkg --printsrcinfo` or `--nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` poses no risk of arbitrary code execution or data exfiltration.

All directives are static and declarative, with no network activity or shell expansions occurring at parse time. The source origin is the official and expected GitHub release page for the project.
</details>
<summary>
Safe static source; no runtime code execution
</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- Safe static source; no runtime code execution[/]

LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD fetches a pre-built binary from the official GitHub repository of the project (feather-wallet/feather), specifically from the verified release page for version 2.8.1. The source URL is correctly scoped to the project's own release assets, and the checksum (sha256sums) is provided and matches the expected value for that release. The package installs the binary into /opt/feather and creates a symlink in /usr/bin, both standard practices for binary packages.

There are no signs of malicious behavior: no network calls outside the declared source, no use of eval, base64, or curl|bash patterns, no obfuscation, and no unexpected file operations. The use of bsdtar to extract a ZIP archive and setting execute permissions on the binary are normal for a -bin package. The package only provides and conflicts with expected package names, indicating standard integration.

While the package installs a pre-compiled binary (which inherently limits auditability of the binary itself), this is consistent with the -bin convention in AUR, and the source and checksum match the upstream's official release. There is no evidence of supply-chain tampering or injected malicious logic in the PKGBUILD.
</details>
<summary>
Official binary, proper checksum, no malicious code</summary>
</security_assessment>

Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD contains only variable assignments in the global scope and a `package()` function that will not be executed during `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` or `makepkg --printsrcinfo`. The source is a fixed-release zip from the official Feather Wallet GitHub repository with a pinned sha256 checksum, which is standard and not indicative of any malicious behavior. There is no top-level code execution, no pkgver() function, and no dangerous commands (curl, eval, etc.) in the sourced scope. The commands in question are safe to run.
</details>
<summary>Safe: standard PKGBUILD, no top-level execution risks.</summary>
</security_assessment>

The `package()` function extracts the archive, makes the binary executable, and creates a symlink in `/usr/bin`. There are no suspicious network requests, obfuscated commands, or unexpected system modifications. No red flags for supply-chain attacks.
</details>

Audit Skips:

/tmp/aur-sleuth/bulk-reports/deepseek-deepseek-v4-flash/aur-sleuth-28n1t7h1/feather-2.8.1-linux.zip: [SKIPPED] Skipping binary file: feather-2.8.1-linux.zip

data.reasoning: The package is a standard AUR binary package. The PKGBUILD fetches a precompiled release ZIP from the official Feather Wallet GitHub release page with a pinned SHA256 checksum (not SKIP), uses noextract, and the package() function only extracts the archive, sets execute permissions, and creates a symlink in /usr/bin. There is no top-level code execution, no pkgver() function, no network calls beyond the declared source, and no obfuscation or curl|bash patterns. Both models correctly converged on SAFE with consistent, accurate reasoning. The only unreviewed item is the binary itself, which is inherent and unavoidable for a -bin package.
coverage_issues: The binary ZIP (feather-2.8.1-linux.zip) was skipped by both models — this is the expected and correct behavior since binary blobs cannot be meaningfully analyzed by an LLM, but it means the audit cannot vouch for the contents of the binary itself, only the packaging logic.
learnings: For -bin packages, the audit scope is inherently limited to the PKGBUILD/install scripts; the trust anchor is the pinned checksum + official upstream URL, which both models correctly identified. The tool could explicitly note this limitation in -bin verdicts rather than treating the skipped binary as a coverage gap.

Both models reviewed the only auditable file (PKGBUILD) and reached the same correct conclusion — the 'shallow' trigger (1 file reviewed) was a false alarm here, since the repo legitimately contains only a PKGBUILD plus a binary blob. The trigger heuristic could account for package type (-bin packages naturally have few auditable files).

The checksum was verified to not use 'SKIP' — this is the key safety signal for binary packages and was correctly emphasized. The tool should continue prioritizing checksum integrity checks for -bin packages.
No .install hook, prepare(), build(), or check() functions were present; both models verified absence of top-level execution. Coverage was complete for the auditable surface.

data.reasoning: PKGBUILD uses pinned checksum for official GitHub release, contains no dangerous patterns (curl|bash, eval, etc.), and only standard installation logic. Binary skipping is expected limitation.
coverage_issues: Both models skipped binary file (feather-2.8.1-linux.zip) which contains the actual executable
No review of extracted binary contents for runtime behaviors (filesystem/network access)
false_negatives: Potential binary risks: Models didn't analyze extracted wallet executable for suspicious behaviors (crypto-mining, data exfiltration)
learnings: Tool should prioritize executable binaries over archives when present
Implement basic binary analysis (strings output review) for common red flags
Clarify in reports that binary safety depends on upstream trust
Improve handling of zip contents: PKGBUILD extracts to /opt/feather - these files warrant review
